categories.label: Building Envelope
categories.description: The physical barriers between conditioned interior space and the outside environment.

items.label: Attic Insulation
items.guidance: Insulating your attic is one of the most cost-effective ways to reduce heat loss in winter and heat gain in summer. Proper attic insulation can cut heating and cooling costs significantly. Even adding insulation on top of existing material improves performance.

items.label: Wall Insulation
items.guidance: Walls account for a large portion of a home's heat loss. Adding insulation inside wall cavities or as an exterior layer dramatically reduces energy use year-round. The method depends on your wall type and budget.

items.label: Basement / Foundation Insulation
items.guidance: Insulating basement walls or the rim joist reduces cold floors and heat loss through the foundation. It is a relatively affordable upgrade that improves comfort noticeably in colder climates.

items.label: Windows
items.guidance: Old or single-pane windows lose significant heat in winter and admit unwanted solar heat in summer. Upgrading to double or triple-pane windows with low-emissivity coatings cuts energy use and improves comfort near windows.

items.label: Exterior Doors
items.guidance: Poorly insulated or ill-fitting exterior doors let cold air in and warm air out. Replacing an old door with an insulated steel or fibreglass door, or adding weatherstripping, makes a quick difference to comfort and energy bills.

items.label: Air Sealing
items.guidance: Uncontrolled air leaks through gaps, cracks, and penetrations can account for 25–40% of heating and cooling losses. Sealing these leaks with caulk, spray foam, and weatherstripping is low-cost and high-impact.

items.label: Roofing Material
items.guidance: When replacing your roof, choosing a cool or reflective roofing material reduces solar heat gain in warm climates. Metal and light-coloured surfaces reflect more sunlight than dark asphalt shingles, lowering cooling loads.

items.label: Crawlspace Insulation
items.guidance: An uninsulated crawlspace allows cold, damp air to chill floors above and can increase heating costs substantially. Insulating the floor above the crawlspace or sealing and conditioning the crawlspace itself prevents moisture problems and improves comfort.

categories.label: Heating & Cooling
categories.description: Systems that control indoor temperature, including furnaces, heat pumps, air conditioning, and ventilation.

items.label: Furnace / Boiler
items.guidance: Your heating system is often the single largest energy user in your home. Upgrading from an old inefficient furnace to a modern high-efficiency unit or heat pump can dramatically reduce energy use and carbon emissions.

items.label: Air-Source Heat Pump
items.guidance: Air-source heat pumps move heat rather than generate it, making them two to four times more efficient than electric resistance heating. Modern cold-climate models work effectively even in very cold temperatures.

items.label: Ground-Source Heat Pump
items.guidance: Ground-source (geothermal) heat pumps use stable underground temperatures to deliver very high efficiency heating and cooling year-round. They require significant upfront investment and site assessment but deliver outstanding long-term savings.

items.label: Air Conditioning
items.guidance: If you use air conditioning, choosing a high-efficiency unit with a good SEER rating reduces electricity consumption significantly. Proper sizing and maintenance are just as important as efficiency ratings.

items.label: Heat Recovery Ventilator (HRV/ERV)
items.guidance: As homes become more airtight, controlled ventilation with heat recovery becomes essential. An HRV or ERV exchanges stale indoor air for fresh outdoor air while recovering 70–85% of the heat, keeping energy losses low.

items.label: Smart Thermostat
items.guidance: A smart or programmable thermostat lets you reduce heating and cooling when the house is empty or everyone is asleep. Many models learn your schedule automatically and can save 10–15% on heating and cooling bills.

items.label: Duct Sealing
items.guidance: Leaky ducts in forced-air heating and cooling systems can waste 20–30% of the conditioned air before it reaches living spaces. Sealing ducts with mastic or foil tape pays back quickly and improves comfort throughout the house.

categories.label: Hot Water
categories.description: Systems for heating domestic hot water, which is typically the second or third largest energy end use in a home.

items.label: Tank Water Heater
items.guidance: Water heating accounts for roughly 15–20% of home energy use. If you have an older tank water heater, replacing it with a high-efficiency model or heat pump water heater can substantially cut energy costs.

items.label: Tankless (On-Demand) Water Heater
items.guidance: Tankless water heaters heat water only when needed, eliminating standby losses from a storage tank. They can be more efficient than conventional tanks, especially for smaller households with lower hot water demand.

items.label: Heat Pump Water Heater
items.guidance: A heat pump water heater uses electricity to move heat from surrounding air into the water, making it two to three times more efficient than a standard electric resistance heater. It works best in unconditioned spaces such as basements or garages.

items.label: Solar Thermal Hot Water
items.guidance: Solar thermal collectors on the roof preheat water before it enters your hot water system, reducing the energy needed to reach the desired temperature. In sunny climates, a solar thermal system can provide 50–80% of domestic hot water needs.

categories.label: Renewable Energy
categories.description: On-site generation and storage of clean energy, plus connection to renewable electricity programmes.

items.label: Solar Photovoltaic (PV) Panels
items.guidance: Solar PV panels convert sunlight into electricity and are one of the most impactful upgrades for reducing energy bills and carbon emissions. Costs have dropped dramatically in recent years and payback periods are often 5–10 years.

items.label: Battery Storage
items.guidance: Home battery systems store excess solar electricity for use at night or during outages. They increase self-consumption of solar power and provide resilience when the grid goes down.

items.label: EV Charging
items.guidance: If you drive or plan to drive an electric vehicle, installing a dedicated Level 2 home charger allows overnight charging at a convenient rate. Pairing EV charging with solar can eliminate most transport fuel costs.

items.label: Small Wind Turbine
items.guidance: Small wind turbines can complement solar generation in windy rural locations. They require sufficient wind resources, setback space, and local permits, making them suitable only for select properties.

items.label: Green Power Tariff
items.guidance: Many utilities offer green electricity tariffs that supply power from certified renewable sources. This is often the lowest-cost way to reduce the carbon footprint of your electricity use without installing generation equipment.

categories.label: Water Efficiency
categories.description: Reducing indoor and outdoor water consumption through efficient fixtures, harvesting, and landscape choices.

items.label: Low-Flow Taps & Showerheads
items.guidance: Installing low-flow aerators on taps and a water-efficient showerhead is one of the cheapest and quickest water-saving upgrades. Modern low-flow showerheads maintain good pressure while using significantly less water.

items.label: Low-Flow Toilets
items.guidance: Toilets are the largest indoor water user in most homes. Replacing an old high-volume toilet with a modern dual-flush or ultra-low-flow model can cut toilet water use by 50% or more.

items.label: Rainwater Harvesting
items.guidance: Collecting roof runoff in rain barrels or cisterns provides free water for garden irrigation and, where permitted, toilet flushing. Even a simple rain barrel connected to a downspout can capture hundreds of litres per rainfall event.

items.label: Greywater Reuse
items.guidance: Greywater from sinks, showers, and laundry can be treated and reused for toilet flushing or outdoor irrigation, significantly reducing mains water demand. Systems range from simple laundry-to-landscape setups to whole-house treatment systems.

items.label: Drought-Tolerant Landscaping
items.guidance: Replacing water-hungry lawns and plants with native or drought-tolerant species can reduce outdoor water use by 50–75%. Mulching garden beds also retains soil moisture and reduces the need for irrigation.

items.label: Smart Irrigation
items.guidance: Smart irrigation controllers adjust watering schedules based on local weather data and soil moisture sensors, preventing overwatering. They can reduce outdoor water use by 30–50% compared to fixed-timer systems.

categories.label: Lighting & Appliances
categories.description: Efficient lighting, major appliances, and smart controls that reduce plug load and standby consumption.

items.label: LED Lighting
items.guidance: LED bulbs use 75–80% less electricity than incandescent bulbs and last 15–25 times longer. Replacing all bulbs in your home with LEDs is one of the fastest payback upgrades available.

items.label: Efficient Refrigerator
items.guidance: Refrigerators run 24 hours a day and older models can consume two to three times the electricity of a modern energy-efficient unit. When replacing a fridge, look for the most efficient model in the right size for your household.

items.label: Efficient Clothes Washer
items.guidance: Front-loading and high-efficiency top-loading washing machines use significantly less water and energy than older agitator-style machines. Washing in cold water further reduces energy use with no loss of cleaning performance for most loads.

items.label: Efficient Dishwasher
items.guidance: A modern energy-efficient dishwasher uses less water and energy per wash than handwashing dishes. Running full loads and using the air-dry setting rather than heat drying reduces energy use further.

items.label: Smart Power Strips
items.guidance: Many electronics draw power even when switched off or in standby mode. Smart power strips automatically cut power to devices that are not in use, eliminating this standby or 'phantom' load throughout the home.
